label: ai
topic: Review of a psychological horror film with Hugh Grant as a sinister intellectual
text:
There's something inherently unsettling about politeness stretched to its breaking point, and this psychological horror film understands that better than most. Centered on two young Mormon missionaries who find themselves trapped in the home of an unsettlingly articulate man played by Hugh Grant, the film thrives in its first half on tension that feels almost unbearable—not because of overt violence, but because of the quiet, creeping realization that something is deeply wrong.

Grant's performance is the film's undeniable anchor. Known primarily for his charm, he weaponizes that familiarity here, delivering lines with a measured calm that makes them all the more disturbing. In an early scene, he invites the missionaries in under the pretense of a theological discussion, his tone warm and inviting. But as the conversation progresses, subtle shifts in his demeanor—slightly prolonged eye contact, a smile that lingers just a beat too long—create an undercurrent of menace that never fully dissipates.

The dialogue in these early sequences is exceptional. The film takes its time, allowing conversations about faith, doubt, and morality to unfold with a natural rhythm. One particularly striking scene involves Grant's character posing a series of hypothetical questions about belief, each one more unsettling than the last. The missionaries, initially confident in their responses, begin to falter, their certainty eroding under the weight of his relentless probing. It's a masterclass in building tension through language rather than action.

Visually, the film complements this slow-burn approach. The house itself becomes a character, its narrow hallways and dimly lit rooms creating a sense of claustrophobia. The camera often lingers in doorways or frames characters through partially closed spaces, reinforcing the idea that something is always being hidden, just out of view. A scene in which one of the missionaries attempts to leave, only to find every exit subtly blocked, is particularly effective in conveying a sense of entrapment without resorting to overt spectacle.

However, as the film transitions into its second half, it begins to lose some of the nuance that made it so compelling. The psychological tension gives way to more conventional horror elements, including a series of increasingly predictable twists and a climactic sequence that feels at odds with the film's earlier restraint. Without giving too much away, the final act leans heavily on physical confrontation and explicit revelations, abandoning the ambiguity that had made the earlier scenes so effective.

This shift is most evident in how the film handles Grant's character. In the first half, he is terrifying precisely because he is unknowable, his motivations obscured beneath layers of intellectualism and charm. By the end, however, the film attempts to explain him in ways that feel reductive, stripping away some of the complexity that made him so compelling.

Despite these shortcomings, the film remains a strong entry in the psychological horror genre. The performances of the two missionaries are also worth noting, particularly in how they convey the gradual unraveling of their characters' composure. Their fear is not immediate but accumulative, building as each interaction with Grant's character chips away at their sense of control.

Ultimately, this is a film that excels in its ability to create tension through dialogue and performance, even if it falters in its resolution. It may not fully stick the landing, but the journey—particularly its first half—is more than worth the price of admission.
